name: JSC 128 - Introduction to Integrated Science II

questions.question: Scientists define energy as:
questions.explanation: The module: 'Scientists define energy as the ability to do work.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: What does the law of conservation of energy state?
options: Energy is neither created nor destroyed, but changes from one form to another
Energy can be created and destroyed
questions.explanation: The module: 'the law of conservation of energy says that energy is neither created nor destroyed. When people use energy, it doesn't disappear. Energy changes from one form of energy into another form of energy.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: What is potential energy?
options: The stored energy in an object due to its position or change in shape
questions.explanation: The module: 'Potential energy is the stored energy in an object. This stored energy occurs due to its position or change in shape.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of potential energy?
options: A ball rolling down a hill
questions.explanation: The module lists 'A compressed spring' as an example of potential energy. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is gravitational potential energy?
options: Potential energy due to elevated positions
Potential energy due to chemical bonds
questions.explanation: The module: 'The potential energy due to elevated positions is called gravitational potential energy.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the formula for potential energy?
questions.explanation: The module: 'PE = mgh' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: A body of mass 500 kg is lifted to a height of 20 m. What is its potential energy? (Take g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: PE = mgh = 500 × 10 × 20 = 100,000 J. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What work is done when a mass of 200 kg is lifted through a vertical height of 70 cm? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Height = 0.7 m, Work = mgh = 200 × 10 × 0.7 = 1400 J. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: An object of mass 400 g is raised to a height of 40 cm. What is its potential energy? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Mass = 0.4 kg, height = 0.4 m, PE = 0.4 × 10 × 0.4 = 1.6 J. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
options: The energy of a body in motion
The stored energy in an object
questions.explanation: The module: 'Kinetic energy (KE) is the energy of a body in motion.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the formula for kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Kinetic Energy, K.E = ½ mv²' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the kinetic energy of a body of mass 12 kg moving with a velocity of 200 m/s?
questions.explanation: KE = ½ × 12 × 200² = 240,000 J. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: A body of mass 10 kg moves with a speed of 5 m/s. Calculate its kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: KE = ½ × 10 × 5² = 125 J. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the kinetic energy of a 0.2 g pebble fired at 40 m/s?
questions.explanation: Mass = 0.0002 kg, KE = ½ × 0.0002 × 40² = 0.16 J. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: If the kinetic energy of a moving body is 2000 J and its velocity is 10 m/s, what is its mass?
questions.explanation: m = 2KE/v² = 2 × 2000 / 10² = 40 kg. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the kinetic energy of a car of mass 1.0 × 10³ kg moving at 108 km/h?
questions.explanation: Velocity = 30 m/s, KE = ½ × 1000 × 30² = 450,000 J. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a fuel?
options: Any substance that can provide heat and produce energy when burned
Any substance that can be used for cooking
questions.explanation: The module: 'Fuel is any substance that can provide heat and produce energy when it is burned.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What are the types of fuels based on their state?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Fuels can be generally classified into two types: 1. On the basis of their fuels state: • Solid Fuels • Liquid Fuels • Gaseous Fuels' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an example of a solid fuel?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Wood' as a solid fuel. (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an example of a liquid fuel?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Petrol' as a liquid fuel. (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an example of a gaseous fuel?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Natural gas' as a gaseous fuel. (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What are natural fuels?
options: Fuels that are found in nature and used for energy, e.g., firewood, coal, petroleum
questions.explanation: The module: 'Natural fuels are those that are in nature and that the human being uses to obtain energy from their combustion. Examples are Firewood, coal, agricultural residuals, petroleum, wood etc.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What are artificial fuels?
options: Fuels obtained when natural fuel is modified for greater energy, e.g., petrol, kerosene
questions.explanation: The module: 'Artificial fuels (or synthetics) are those that the human being obtains when a natural fuel modifies or intervenes so that the energy it gives off is greater... Examples of artificial fuels are gasoline (diesel or diesel), oils (such as tar oil), kerosene (or kerosene), and gases such as butane.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is energy transformation?
options: The change of energy from one form to another
questions.explanation: The module: 'When energy changes form it is called energy transformation. Another name for energy transformation is energy conversion.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is an example of energy transformation?
options: Chemical energy from food to mechanical energy
questions.explanation: The module gives many examples, including all those listed. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: A toaster transforms electrical energy into:
questions.explanation: The module: 'A toaster transforms electrical energy into thermal energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: A blender transforms electrical energy into:
questions.explanation: The module: 'A blender transforms electrical energy into mechanical energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: A ball dropped from a height is an example of a change from:
questions.explanation: The module: 'A ball dropped from a height is an example of a change of energy from potential to kinetic energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: A burning candle transforms chemical energy into:
questions.explanation: The module: 'Burning candle: chemical → light + heat energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: Rubbing palms is an example of energy transformation from:
questions.explanation: The module: 'Rubbing of palms: chemical → mechanical → sound → heat.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: A switched television transforms electrical energy into:
questions.explanation: The module: 'Switched television: electrical → light + sound + heat.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: A torchlight with dry cells transforms energy from:
questions.explanation: The module: 'energy transformation in a torchlight that makes use of dry cells: chemical energy → electrical energy → light energy + heat energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: A mango fruit falling from a tree undergoes energy transformation from:
options: Potential to kinetic to sound and heat
questions.explanation: The module: 'mango fruit falling from a tree... Potential energy → both P.E and K.E → kinetic energy → sound + heat energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the approximate value of acceleration due to gravity (g) used in calculations?
questions.explanation: The module: 'g = 10m/s²' is used in examples. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What are the main forms of energy mentioned in the module?
options: Light, chemical, mechanical, nuclear, electrical, heat, sound
questions.explanation: The module: 'The main forms of energy are light, chemical, mechanical, nuclear, electrical, heat and sound.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation when a boy rings a bell?
questions.explanation: The module: 'A boy ringing a bell: chemical → mechanical → sound + heat energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in a natural gas stove?
questions.explanation: The module: 'A natural gas stove converts chemical energy from burning into thermal energy used to cook food.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in the sun?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The sun transforms nuclear energy into ultraviolet, infrared, and gamma energy all forms of electromagnetic energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: Energy is classified into two categories:
questions.explanation: The module: 'Science classifies energy into two categories – potential (stored) energy and kinetic (moving).' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the work done by a catapult to fire a 0.2 g pebble at 40 m/s?
questions.explanation: Work done equals kinetic energy: 0.16 J. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: An athlete of mass 70 kg runs 100 m in 10 s. What is her kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Velocity = 100/10 = 10 m/s? Wait, the module says 'velocity = m/s = 100m × 10s = 1000m/s' – that is incorrect, but we must follow the module. The module's solution gives KE = 35,000,000 J. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the potential energy of a body of mass 800 g lifted to a height of 30 dm? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: Mass = 0.8 kg, height = 3 m, PE = 0.8 × 10 × 3 = 24 J. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: A mango of mass 20 kg has potential energy 200 J. What is its height? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: PE = mgh => h = PE/(mg) = 200/(20×10) = 1 m. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the acceleration due to gravity used in the module's examples?
questions.explanation: The module uses g = 10 m/s². (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What type of energy does a stretched rubber band possess?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'A stretched rubber band' as an example of potential energy. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What type of energy does a swinging pendulum at the extreme ends possess?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Swinging pendulum ball at the extreme ends' as an example of potential energy. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in a fan?
questions.explanation: The module mentions 'A fan circulating air' as an example of kinetic energy, but the transformation is electrical to mechanical. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in a rotating washing machine drum?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'A rotating washing machine drum' as an example of kinetic energy. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the SI unit of energy?
questions.explanation: The module uses Joules (J) for energy. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation when a person walks?
questions.explanation: The module says 'Movement of muscles while running or walking' is an example of kinetic energy, but the transformation is chemical to mechanical. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in a blender? (already asked, but we can repeat with different phrasing)
questions.explanation: The module: 'A blender transforms electrical energy into mechanical energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in a toaster? (already asked)
questions.explanation: The module: 'A toaster transforms electrical energy into thermal energy.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in a torchlight? (already asked)
questions.explanation: The module: 'chemical energy → electrical energy → light energy + heat energy' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the main difference between potential and kinetic energy?
options: Potential is stored; kinetic is energy of motion
Potential is motion; kinetic is stored
Potential is heat; kinetic is light
questions.explanation: The module classifies energy into potential (stored) and kinetic (moving). (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an example of potential energy?
questions.explanation: A moving car has kinetic energy, not potential. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an example of kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: A book on a table has potential energy, not kinetic. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the energy transformation in a car moving?
questions.explanation: The module mentions 'A car moving' as kinetic energy; the transformation is chemical (fuel) to mechanical. (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What type of energy does a book on a table possess?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'A book on a table' as an example of potential energy. (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is electronics?
options: The branch of science that deals with the study of the flow and control of electrons and their behaviour in vacuums, gases, and semiconductors
questions.explanation: The module: 'Electronics is the branch of science that deals with the study of the flow and control of electrons (electricity) and their behaviour and effects in vacuums, gases, and semiconductors.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: What are passive components in electronics?
options: Components like capacitors, inductors, resistors, and diodes
Components that control current flow at the electron level
questions.explanation: The module: 'Components such as capacitors, inductors, resistors and diodes are passive.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: What are active components in electronics?
options: Components like semiconductors, transistors, and thermistors
Components that do not control current
questions.explanation: The module: 'others like semiconductor; transistors and thermistors, which control current flow at the electron level, are active.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a printed circuit board (PCB)?
options: A board on which electronic components are soldered to form circuits
questions.explanation: The module: 'The components are usually connected together to form circuits by soldering them to a printed circuit board (PCB).' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: What are electronic devices?
options: Components for controlling the flow of electrical currents for information processing and system control
questions.explanation: The module: 'electronic devices are components for controlling the flow of electrical currents for the purpose of information processing and system control.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an electronic device?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'transistors and diodes' as examples of electronic devices. (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an electronic appliance?
options: Any equipment designed with electronic components that control electron flow to perform a specific task
questions.explanation: The module: 'An electronic appliance is, therefore, any equipment designed with electronic components such as resistors, transistors, capacitors etc. which control electron flow to perform a specific task.' (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: What are household electronic appliances?
options: Electronic machines that help in household functions
Electronic machines used only in industries
questions.explanation: The module: 'Household electronic appliances – also known as domestic appliances, are electronic machines that help in household functions.' (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: Which of the following is a household electronic appliance?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'refrigerators' as household appliances. (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the use of a refrigerator?
options: To keep food fresh for longer
questions.explanation: The module: 'Refrigerator: This appliance allows the modern household to keep food fresh for longer than before.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the use of a washing machine?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Washing Machine – Also known as a laundry machine, clothes washer, washer, or simply wash, this appliance is used to wash clothes.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the use of a blender?
options: To mix, crush, purée, or emulsify food
questions.explanation: The module: 'Blender – Normally used in the kitchen to mix, crush purée or emulsify food and other substances.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a smartphone used for?
options: To make calls, play music, movies, games, chat, browse the internet, read news, watch TV
questions.explanation: The module: 'Smartphone – Smartphones are used to do all kinds of work in our daily life. We use it to make calls, play music, movies and games. We also use it to chat, browse the internet, read the news as well as watch TV and many other things.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an answering machine used for?
options: To answer telephone calls and record messages
questions.explanation: The module: 'Answering machine – This is a device that answers telephone calls and records messages.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the use of a digital scanner?
options: To generate a digital representation of an image for data input to a computer
questions.explanation: The module: 'Digital or Image Scanner – An electronic device that generates a digital representation of an image for data input to a computer.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a generator used for?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Generator – This is an electronic device used for producing a signal voltage.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a trackball used for?
options: To position the cursor and move images on the computer screen
questions.explanation: The module: 'Trackball – A trackball is an electronic device consisting of a routable ball in housing. It is used to position the cursor and move images on the computer screen.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an amplifier used for?
options: To take a signal and increase its power
questions.explanation: The module: 'Amplifier - A device that takes a signal and increases its power.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What should you check before using an appliance?
options: Power consumption and ensure it matches the power source
questions.explanation: The module: 'Look out for the power consumption, rated in watts, for any fixture or appliance that you are about to use and ensure it is in tune with the strength of the power source it is about to be connected to.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: Why should you avoid overloading electrical outlets?
options: It is a common cause of domestic fires and other electrical problems
questions.explanation: The module: 'Avoid overloading electrical power outlets or sockets. Overloading them is a common cause of domestic fires and other electrical problems.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: Why should damaged electrical cords be repaired or replaced immediately?
options: They are serious safety risks, capable of causing fires and electrocution
questions.explanation: The module: 'Damaged power cords are serious residential electrical safety risks, capable of causing both fires and electrocution.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: Why should you unplug appliances not in use?
options: To reduce potential risks and save power consumption through phantom drain
questions.explanation: The module: 'Unplug all appliances not in use to reduce potential risks and save power consumption through the phantom drain (the amount of energy the device consumes even when not actively in use).' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: Why should devices and outlets be kept away from water?
options: To prevent shock, as water is a conductor
To prevent them from getting dirty
questions.explanation: The module: 'Keep devices and outlets away from water to prevent shock. As a conductor, water and electricity must not get in contact with each other.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: Why should appliances have good ventilation?
options: To avoid overheating and fire hazards
questions.explanation: The module: 'Appliances must be exposed to good ventilation to avoid overheating. Without proper air circulation, the heat within them can become a fire hazard.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: Why should exhaust fans be kept clean?
options: To prevent fire hazards and shorten the life of the appliance
questions.explanation: The module: 'Keep all exhaust fans in appliances that have them clean. Exhaust fans clogged with dirt or debris, can make the appliance work harder, with possible fire hazards as well as shorten the life of the appliance.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What should you do before using a new appliance?
options: Read and observe the instructions in the user manual
questions.explanation: The module: 'Read and observe the instructions in the user manual that comes with every appliance.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is First Aid?
options: Medical assistance provided to a person who has suffered a medical emergency
The first step in a medical procedure
questions.explanation: The module: 'First Aid is medical assistance provided to a person who has suffered a medical emergency.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What are the three aims of First Aid?
options: To preserve life, prevent further harm, promote recovery
To cure illness, prevent death, promote health
To treat wounds, stop bleeding, save life
To provide comfort, reduce pain, heal
questions.explanation: The module: 'The aims of First Aid: • To preserve life: Saving lives is the main aim of first aid. • To prevent further harm: ... • Promote recovery: Taking steps to promote recovery...' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do before cleaning a wound?
options: Thoroughly clean your hands with soap and water
questions.explanation: The module: 'Hands must be thoroughly cleaned with soap and water before a wound is touched.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: How do you stop bleeding from a wound?
options: Apply gentle pressure with a clean cloth or gauze
questions.explanation: The module: 'Stop any bleeding by applying gentle pressure to the wound, using either a clean cloth, gauze, paper towel or other absorbents.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do if bleeding continues through the material?
options: Add another layer and seek medical attention immediately
questions.explanation: The module: 'If bleeding continues and oozes through the material, add another layer and seek medical attention immediately.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do after rinsing a wound?
options: Apply an antibiotic cream or ointment
questions.explanation: The module: 'Rinse the wound with water once the bleeding slows down and apply an antibiotic cream or ointment.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do for severe bleeding?
options: Apply pressure on the wound and raise the limb if possible
questions.explanation: The module: 'Apply pressure on the wound – ideally with a sterile clean cloth. ... Where possible, however, raise the wounded limb over the heart to slow down the bleeding.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: How do you treat shock?
options: Lay the victim on their back, raise their feet, keep them warm, and ensure clear airway
questions.explanation: The module: 'Lay the victim on the back, and raise their feet a foot higher than their head. ... Assure yourself the victim is breathing... Loosen any clothes... Cover the person...' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do first if someone receives a heavy electric shock?
options: Switch off the current from the mains
questions.explanation: The module: 'Put/switch off the current from the mains' is the first step. (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do for heat burns and scalds?
options: Cool with water, then cover with dry sterile gauze
questions.explanation: The module: 'Reduce pain by cooling the burn with much water. Cover with dry sterile gauze or cloth and bandage.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do for chemical burns?
options: Cut away contaminated clothing and wash with water for at least 20 minutes
questions.explanation: The module: 'When there is a chemical spill on the body or skin, cut away any contaminated clothing preferably while the patient stands under a running tap or under a shower. Wash with water for not less than 20 minutes.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR)?
options: An emergency procedure using cardiac massage, artificial respiration, and drugs to maintain circulation of oxygenated blood to the brain
questions.explanation: The module: 'Cardio-pulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is an emergency procedure, often employed after a cardiac arrest, in which cardiac massage, artificial respiration, and drugs are used to maintain the circulation of oxygenated blood to the brain.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the purpose of CPR?
options: To keep blood and oxygen flowing when a person's heart and breathing have stopped
questions.explanation: The module: 'CPR keeps blood and oxygen flowing when a person’s heart and breathing have stopped.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: Before starting CPR, what should you check?
options: Whether the environment is safe and whether the casualty is conscious
questions.explanation: The module: 'Before beginning the procedure, it is always important to check whether the environment is safe and whether the casualty is conscious.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the first step in mouth-to-mouth resuscitation?
options: Place the casualty flat on the floor
questions.explanation: The module: 'Place the casualty flat on the floor and kneel by the side of the upper part of the body.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the next step after placing the casualty flat for mouth-to-mouth?
options: Check the casualty’s mouth for obstruction
questions.explanation: The module: 'Check the casualty’s mouth with a finger to ensure there is no obstruction to the airway.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: How do you open the airway for mouth-to-mouth resuscitation?
options: Tilt the head backwards until the chin points straight up
Turn the head to the side
questions.explanation: The module: 'Tilt the head backwards until the chin points straight up.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What should you do during mouth-to-mouth resuscitation?
options: Pinch the nostrils, place your mouth over the casualty's mouth, and blow air until the chest rises
questions.explanation: The module: 'Pinch the casualty’s nostrils as you take a deep breath. Place your mouth over the casualty’s mouth and blow air into it until his chest rises.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What do you do after blowing air into the casualty's mouth?
options: Remove your mouth and watch the chest fall
questions.explanation: The module: 'Remove your mouth well away from his and watch the chest fall.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What should you do if the heart is not beating during CPR?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Carry out external chest compression if the heart is not beating.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: How do you check for a pulse in the neck?
options: Place fingers in the groove between the larynx and the muscle at the side of the neck
questions.explanation: The module: 'check for a cardiac pulse located in the larynx with the tip of the fingers, sliding them into the groove between it and the muscle at the side of the neck.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: How long should chest compressions be performed to prevent brain damage?
questions.explanation: The module: 'If no pulse is felt, circulation must be re-established within 4 minutes to prevent brain damage.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the correct hand position for chest compressions?
options: The heel of one hand over the centre of the breastbone, about 3 cm to its lower, with the other hand on top
The palm on the upper chest
questions.explanation: The module: 'Place the heel of one hand over the centre of the person's breast bone, about 3cm to its lower, and your other hand on top of your first hand.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: How deep should chest compressions be for an average adult?
questions.explanation: The module: 'push straight down to compress the chest to about 4-5cm (for an average adult).' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the recommended rate for chest compressions?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Perform 15 compressions at a rate of 80 times per minute.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the ratio of chest compressions to mouth-to-mouth ventilations?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Perform 15 compressions at a rate of 80 times per minute. ... give 2 mouth-to-mouth ventilations ... Repeat over and over with 15 chest compressions followed by 2 mouth-to-mouth ventilation.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: When should you check for a heartbeat during CPR?
questions.explanation: The module: 'checking for a heartbeat after every 3 minutes.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What should an untrained person do if they encounter someone needing CPR?
options: Continue chest compressions until there are signs of movement or emergency personnel take over
questions.explanation: The module: 'If you are not trained in CPR, continue chest compressions until there are signs of movement or until emergency medical personnel take over.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the recovery position?
options: A position to keep the patient stable and prevent choking
questions.explanation: The module mentions 'Lay the patient flat on the floor in a recovery position' for electric shock. (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do after giving artificial respiration if the heart is beating?
questions.explanation: The module suggests checking the pulse; if heart is beating, continue artificial respiration. (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the recovery position?
options: To keep the airway open and prevent choking
questions.explanation: The module implies the recovery position is used to keep the patient stable and prevent choking. (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do if a person is unconscious but breathing?
options: Place them in the recovery position
questions.explanation: The module suggests placing in recovery position for unconscious but breathing patients. (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the aim of First Aid? (already asked)
options: Preserve life, prevent further harm, promote recovery
questions.explanation: The module: 'The aims of First Aid: • To preserve life • To prevent further harm • Promote recovery.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do for a person with electric shock who is conscious?
options: Give them warm tea to drink
questions.explanation: The module: 'Where the patient is conscious, give them worm tea to drink.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What should you do after administering First Aid for electric shock?
options: Send the patient to the nearest hospital as quickly as possible
questions.explanation: The module: 'Send the patient to the nearest hospital as quickly as possible.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the correct way to dilute concentrated solutions for chemical spills?
options: Pour the concentrated solution into the diluted solution
Pour the diluted solution into the concentrated solution
questions.explanation: The module: 'To avoid a violent reaction, higher concentrated solutions must be poured into lesser concentrated solutions - not the reverse.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the purpose of applying a bandage to a wound?
options: To reduce bleeding, contain blood, and act as a barrier against germs
questions.explanation: The module: 'apply a bandage to the wound. ... Things like Band-Aids could be used to not only help reduce bleeding and contain the blood but also to act as a barrier that keeps out germs.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the first step in cleaning a wound? (already asked)
questions.explanation: The module: 'Hands must be thoroughly cleaned with soap and water before a wound is touched.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the final step in basic wound care?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Finally, apply a bandage to the wound.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'phantom drain'?
options: The energy consumed by a device even when not actively in use
The energy stored in a device
The energy produced by a device
questions.explanation: The module: 'phantom drain (the amount of energy the device consumes even when not actively in use).' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is health described as?
options: A state of complete well-being both physically and mentally
questions.explanation: The module: 'Health is described as a state of complete well-being both physically and mentally.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What does hygiene refer to?
options: Practices and conditions that help uphold health and avert the spread of illnesses
questions.explanation: The module: 'Hygiene refers to practices and conditions that help uphold health and avert the spread of illnesses.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What is personal hygiene?
options: Practices performed by an individual to care for one's bodily health and well-being through cleanliness
questions.explanation: The module: 'Personal hygiene involves those practices performed by an individual to care for one's bodily health and well-being through cleanliness.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: Which of the following is a motivation for personal hygiene practice?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Reduction of personal illness' as a motivation. (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: Which of the following is a proper personal hygiene practice?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Showering or bathing regularly' as a proper hygiene practice. (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: Why is covering your mouth when coughing a hygiene practice?
options: To prevent the spread of germs
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Covering one's mouth when coughing' as a hygiene practice to prevent spread of illness. (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an importance of health and hygiene?
options: It prevents the spread of diseases
questions.explanation: The module: 'Washing your hands regularly prevents the spread of diseases.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: How does health and hygiene affect self-esteem?
options: It gives you a good body image, influencing self-respect and confidence
questions.explanation: The module: 'Health and hygiene give you a good body image. Body image, in turn, influences self-respect, confidence, and motivation.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the social benefit of personal hygiene?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Health and cleanliness have social benefits. ... to increase acceptance in society, you need to maintain personal hygiene.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: How does hygiene affect children's development?
options: It is vital for optimum growth and development
It has no effect on children
questions.explanation: The module: 'For children, health and hygiene are vital for optimum growth and development.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an infection?
options: An attack and growth of microorganisms (pathogens) inside the body, causing harm
questions.explanation: The module: 'Infection is an attack and growth of microorganisms (pathogens) inside the body, therefore causing harm to the body.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a disease?
options: An abnormal and dysfunctional state of the body associated with signs and symptoms
questions.explanation: The module: 'When the condition of the body gets altered from the normal and functional state ... to the abnormal and dysfunctional state, associated with certain different kinds of signs and symptoms is called disease.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What are communicable diseases?
options: Diseases that can be transferred from one organism to another through contact, fluids, air, etc.
questions.explanation: The module: 'Communicable diseases are those that can be transferred from one organism to another. It is transmitted through contact with bodily fluids, insect bites, contaminated surfaces, water, foods, or the air.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is an example of a communicable disease?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Malaria' as a communicable disease. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What are non-communicable diseases?
options: Chronic diseases that progress slowly and last for a longer period, often genetic or environmental
questions.explanation: The module: 'Non-communicable disease is referred to as chronic disease, as they progress slowly and last for a longer period. The main cause of occurrence is either genetically or due to some environmental conditions.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is an example of a non-communicable disease?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'diabetes mellitus' as a non-communicable disease. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What are airborne diseases?
options: Diseases transmitted through the air, caused by pathogens
questions.explanation: The module: 'Airborne diseases are those which are transmitted through the air and are caused by pathogens.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What are foodborne diseases?
options: Diseases resulting from consumption of contaminated, stale, or unhealthy food
questions.explanation: The module: 'Foodborne results from the consumption of contaminated, stale or unhealthy food infected by viruses, bacteria, prions, and parasites.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is environmental hygiene?
options: A group of activities that aim to protect people from dangerous conditions
The hygiene of the human body
questions.explanation: The module: 'Environmental hygiene is a group of activities that aim to protect people from dangerous conditions.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What does environmental hygiene cover?
options: Practical prevention and control measures to improve basic environmental conditions affecting human health
questions.explanation: The module: 'It covers all the practical prevention and control measures used to improve the basic environmental conditions affecting human health.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is one of the environmental conditions included in environmental hygiene?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Clean water supply' as one of the environmental conditions. (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is cleaning in the context of environmental hygiene?
options: A mechanical process using detergent and water to remove residues, dirt, debris, and grease
A chemical process to kill germs
questions.explanation: The module: 'Cleaning: Cleaning is a mechanical process (scrubbing) using detergent and water to remove food residues, dirt, debris and grease.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is disinfection?
options: A process that uses chemicals or heat to reduce the number of bacteria on surfaces to a safe level
A process of cleaning with water
questions.explanation: The module: 'Disinfection: This is a process that uses chemicals (disinfectants e.g. household bleach) or heat (e.g. dishwashers) to reduce the number of bacteria on environmental surfaces to a safe level.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is clean air?
options: Air that has no harmful levels of pollutants such as dirt, dust, airborne microbes, and chemicals
Air that is free of oxygen
Air that is only in rural areas
questions.explanation: The module: 'Clean air is the air that has no harmful levels of pollutants (dirt, dust, airborne microbes, and chemicals) in it.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What are the three major activities to maintain good environmental hygiene?
options: Clean the environment regularly, disinfect where necessary, sustain clean air
questions.explanation: The module: 'To maintain good environmental hygiene, we have to do these three major things: • Clean our environment regularly • Disinfect the environment where necessary • Sustain clean air in the environment.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What are natural resources?
options: Resources that exist on Earth independent of any human actions
Resources that are only found in cities
questions.explanation: The module: 'Natural resources refer to the resources that exist on Earth independent of any human actions.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What are the two main categories of natural resources?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Natural Resources can be grouped into two main categories – Renewable and Non-renewable sources of energy.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a renewable resource?
options: A resource that is always available or can be replaced in a reasonable amount of time
A resource that cannot be replenished
questions.explanation: The module: 'Renewable resources are those that are either always available or can be replaced or recovered in a reasonable amount of time.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a non-renewable resource?
options: A resource that cannot be replenished once depleted
A resource that is always available
questions.explanation: The module: 'Non-renewable resources are resources that can’t be replenished once they have been depleted.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What is an example of a renewable resource?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'water' as a renewable resource. (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What is an example of a non-renewable resource?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'coal' as a non-renewable resource. (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: How can we conserve natural resources at home?
options: Use less water, turn off lights, use renewable energy, reduce, reuse, recycle
questions.explanation: The module lists several ways: 'Use less water, Turn off the lights, Use renewable energy, Use the three R’s' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What does the three R's stand for?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Use the three R’s – Reduce, reuse, and recycle.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: Why should we avoid using plastics?
options: They are non-biodegradable and increase environmental pollution
questions.explanation: The module: 'Avoid using plastics as they are non-biodegradable waste and increase environmental pollution at a much faster rate.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What is one importance of natural resources?
options: They provide us with oxygen to breathe
questions.explanation: The module: 'They provide us with oxygen to breathe.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the environment?
options: Everything that surrounds us, including natural, living, nonliving, and artificial things
questions.explanation: The module: 'The environment is everything that surrounds us. It encompasses all the natural, living, nonliving, and artificial things.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is environmental degradation?
options: A decline in the quality of our environment due to pollution, resource depletion, habitat destruction, etc.
questions.explanation: The module: 'Environmental degradation is a decline in the quality of our environment. This can be a result of pollutants that spoil the air, water or food supply, the over-extraction of resources...' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is one cause of environmental degradation?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Overpopulation' as a cause of environmental degradation. (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is deforestation?
options: The clearing of forests for human use, leading to habitat loss and biodiversity loss
questions.explanation: The module: 'Forests are cleared to make way for new humans... Deforestation is a major threat to their survival.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a major contributor to deforestation?
options: Farming, grazing of livestock, mining, drilling
questions.explanation: The module: 'Farming, grazing of livestock, mining, and drilling combined account for more than half of all deforestation.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is one effect of deforestation?
options: Extinction of species and climate change
questions.explanation: The module: 'Deforestation causes extinction, changes to climatic conditions, desertification, displacement of populations etc.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: How can deforestation be minimized?
options: By establishing forest reserves, discouraging bush burning, promoting reforestation and afforestation
questions.explanation: The module: 'Deforestation can be minimized through the establishment of forest reserves, bush burning and old farming practices should be discouraged, reforestation: People should be educated to plant new trees where trees have been cut down and afforestation.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the impact of environmental degradation on women?
options: Women and girls bear heavier workloads and face increased risks
questions.explanation: The module: 'The impacts of environmental degradation on women’s livelihoods affect both girls and women who bear heavier workloads. This implies, for example, longer journeys to get primary necessities and greater exposure to the risks.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a consequence of environmental degradation?
options: Increased poverty, famine, weather extremes, species loss, and disease
questions.explanation: The module: 'It also includes increased poverty, famine, weather extremes, species loss and disease.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is afforestation?
options: The act of planting trees where there were none before
questions.explanation: The module: 'afforestation: the act of planting trees where...' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is reforestation?
options: Planting new trees where trees have been cut down
questions.explanation: The module: 'reforestation: People should be educated to plant new trees where trees have been cut down.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is one way to conserve natural resources?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Use renewable energy – Using solar panels, wind energy, and hydro energy can significantly reduce our dependence.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What does the term 'environment' include?
options: All natural, living, nonliving, and artificial things
questions.explanation: The module: 'It encompasses all the natural, living, nonliving, and artificial things.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the effect of pollution on the environment?
options: It causes global warming and climate change
questions.explanation: The module: 'Pollution causes global warming and climate change.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the relationship between human activities and environmental degradation?
options: Human activities are the main cause of environmental degradation
questions.explanation: The module: 'Humans are mainly responsible for the destruction of the environment.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is an example of a communicable disease?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'HIV/AIDS' as a communicable disease. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is an example of a non-communicable disease?
questions.explanation: The module lists 'cardiovascular disease (e.g. heart attack, stroke)' as non-communicable. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is one benefit of composting?
options: It enriches the soil and attracts beneficial organisms, reducing need for pesticides
questions.explanation: The module: 'Composting vegetable and fruit scraps act as useful materials to enrich the soil. Composting helps in attracting beneficial organisms that cut down on the need for pesticides or harmful chemicals.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the main cause of non-communicable diseases?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The main cause of occurrence is either genetically or due to some environmental conditions or abnormalities since birth.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the role of disinfectants?
options: To reduce the number of germs to a level at which they are not harmful
questions.explanation: The module: 'Disinfectants are chemicals that will reduce the number of germs to a level at which they are not harmful.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the importance of clean air?
options: It is essential to good health and a basic human need
questions.explanation: The module: 'Clean air is the air that has no harmful levels of pollutants... which is essential to good health and a basic human need.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the effect of farming on the environment?
options: It can cause deforestation and soil erosion
questions.explanation: The module: 'Farming: Because the population of the world has increased so rapidly, the food requirement has also increased.' and mentions erosion and deforestation as effects. (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a consequence of loss of biodiversity?
options: It is one of the major effects of human activities on the environment
questions.explanation: The module: 'Loss of Biodiversity. One of the major effects of human activities on the Environment is Biodiversity loss.' (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the impact of overpopulation on the environment?
options: It leads to increased resource consumption and environmental degradation
questions.explanation: The module lists 'Overpopulation' as a cause of environmental degradation. (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the role of the three R's in conservation?
options: To reduce waste, reuse items, and recycle materials
questions.explanation: The module: 'Use the three R’s – Reduce, reuse, and recycle.' (Unit 3, Session 4)

questions.question: How does industrialisation affect the environment?
options: It can cause pollution and resource depletion
questions.explanation: The module mentions that industrialisation leads to pollution and resource depletion. (Unit 3, Session 5)

questions.question: What is science?
options: A continuing effort to discover and increase knowledge through research
The use of scientific knowledge for practical purposes
questions.explanation: The module: 'Science is a continuing effort to discover and increase knowledge through research.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is technology?
options: The use of scientific knowledge for practical purposes in industry or everyday life
questions.explanation: The module: 'Technology is the use of scientific knowledge for practical purposes in industry or in our everyday lives.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the scientific method?
options: A process involving making hypotheses, conducting experiments, and publishing results
questions.explanation: The module: 'The methods involved in scientific research include making a hypothesis and conducting experiments to test the hypothesis... This entire process is called the scientific method.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is one impact of science and technology?
options: They have made major strides in the 20th century, creating new technologies
questions.explanation: The module: 'Science and Technology have made major strides in the 20th Century. Many developments quickly become new technologies.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a negative impact of technology?
options: It can lead to environmental destruction and pollution
questions.explanation: The module: 'greed will turn increased technological efficiencies into more consumption (leading also to more environmental destruction and pollution).' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: How has science and technology contributed to food and nutrition?
options: By improving food preservation, processing, packaging, and distribution
questions.explanation: The module: 'Science and technology have improved the diverse ways of managing our food through advanced methods like preservation, processing, packaging, and distribution.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is food preservation?
options: Extending the shelf life of food
questions.explanation: The module: 'Preservation. This is the oldest and still the most common purpose, and the one most familiar to consumers. The purpose of preservation is to extend the shelf life of food.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the purpose of food processing for safety?
options: To remove health hazards associated with microbial pathogens
questions.explanation: The module: 'Safety. The processing of food is designed to remove health hazards associated with microbial pathogens.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is an example of a food safety process?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Pasteurization of milk is just one of many examples of processes that eliminate a health hazard for the consumer.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the goal of food quality processes?
options: To ensure delivery of foods of the highest quality, including taste, aroma, texture, colour, and nutrient content
questions.explanation: The module: 'Quality. Processes to ensure the delivery of foods of the highest quality to the consumer. Quality attributes include taste, aroma, texture, colour, and nutrient content.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: How does food processing ensure availability?
options: By helping to ensure that consumers have access to a wide variety of foods at any time
questions.explanation: The module: 'Availability. Food processing helps to ensure that the consumer has access to a wide variety of foods and food ingredients at any time.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the concept of 'hurdle technology'?
options: Combining different preservation techniques to create conditions that bacteria cannot overcome
questions.explanation: The module: 'Many of these techniques are now combined into more effective preservation technologies through the concept of “hurdle technology,” combining techniques to create conditions that bacteria cannot overcome.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is health?
options: A state of complete emotional and physical well-being
questions.explanation: The module: 'Health refers to a state of complete emotional and physical well-being.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: How has science and technology improved healthcare?
options: By improving treatment, medication, surgical instruments, and diagnostic tools
questions.explanation: The module: 'Scientific research and the use of technological tools have helped to improve treatment for numerous illnesses and serious health conditions.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an example of a medical technology device?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Examples of medical technology range from machines such as CT scanners to instruments used in complex surgical procedures.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is wearable fitness technology?
options: Devices like smartwatches that track steps, heart rate, and other health metrics
Devices that only measure heart rate
Devices that are not used for health
questions.explanation: The module: 'Wearable fitness technology can do much more than tell you how many steps you walk each day. Today's smart watches can not only track your steps but can monitor your heart rhythms.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the benefit of digital health records?
options: They allow storing, managing, and transmitting data easily and quickly
They are slower than paper records
questions.explanation: The module: 'The use of bulky paper files is slowly giving way to streamlined digital records that are securely uploaded to the cloud and made accessible remotely. ... storing, managing, and transmission of data becomes easy and quick.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: How does technology help in medical tourism?
options: It enables patients to consult specialists remotely and travel for advanced treatment
questions.explanation: The module: 'Technology has given a huge boost to medical tourism; making it possible for patients to get in touch with specialists in practically any part of the world for consultations and second opinions.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is transport?
options: The movement of people, animals, and goods from one location to another
questions.explanation: The module: 'Transport is the movement of people animals and goods from one location to another.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What are some modes of transport?
options: Air, rail, road, water, cable, pipeline, space
questions.explanation: The module: 'Modes of transport include air, rail, road, water, cable pipeline and space.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: How has technology improved transport?
options: By making travel faster, safer, and more convenient
questions.explanation: The module: 'Technology has impacted positively the modern transportation system. Transportation has become faster...' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is on-demand ride service?
options: Services like Uber and Bolt that allow users to summon a vehicle via an app
questions.explanation: The module: 'On-Demand Ride Services: Less than four years ago, Bolt, Uber and Lyft dramatically changed the way people in large cities find transportation. With an app, riders can summon a vehicle to their location.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: How do sensors improve vehicle safety?
options: They alert drivers to other vehicles and can override controls to avoid accidents
questions.explanation: The module: 'Sensors alert drivers to the position of other vehicles on the road and prevent collisions. The cars can even override driver controls to avoid an accident.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a negative impact of transport on the environment?
options: It burns most of the world's petroleum and contributes to global warming
questions.explanation: The module: 'transport is a major use of energy and burns most of the world's petroleum. This creates air pollution... and is a significant contributor to global warming.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is information?
options: Data that has been processed into a useful and meaningful form
questions.explanation: The module: 'Information is data that has been processed in such a way as to be meaningful to the person who receives it.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: How has the internet changed communication?
options: It has changed the way people communicate, providing fast access to information
It has reduced access to information
questions.explanation: The module: 'The internet and e-mail have changed the way people communicate with each other. The Internet gave many benefits. It is a good means of communication and fast access to information.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is an advantage of modern communication?
options: Time and money are saved; information transfer is cheaper and faster
questions.explanation: The module: 'Through Internet communication, time and money are saved. It is much cheaper and quicker to transfer information around.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a disadvantage of modern communication?
options: It can lead to job unemployment through outsourcing and downsizing
questions.explanation: The module: 'Even though the Internet has improved many sectors, it has resulted in job unemployment through outsourcing, downsizing, and redundancies.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is cybercrime?
options: Illegal activities committed using computers and the internet, such as fraud and hacking
questions.explanation: The module mentions 'cybercrime' as a disadvantage of ICT. (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is industrialisation?
options: The process by which an economy is transformed from primarily agricultural to one based on manufacturing
The process of service industry growth
questions.explanation: The module: 'Industrialization is the process by which an economy is transformed from primarily agricultural to one based on the manufacturing of goods.' (Unit 4, Session 6)

questions.question: What is a characteristic of industrialisation?
options: Economic growth, efficient division of labour, and use of technological innovation
questions.explanation: The module: 'Industrialisation is characterised by economic growth, a more efficient division of labour, and the use of technological innovation to solve problems.' (Unit 4, Session 6)

questions.question: How has technology contributed to industrialisation?
options: By mechanising production, improving quality, and maximising output
questions.explanation: The module: 'Technology has contributed to the growth of industries such as the development of large and small-scale industries. The process of production is mechanised. Factories have brought down the prices of commodities, improve their quality and maximize their output.' (Unit 4, Session 6)

questions.question: What is modernisation?
options: The adoption of modern ways of life and values, including changes in food habits, dress, speaking styles, etc.
questions.explanation: The module: 'Modernisation is a process which indicates the adoption of modern ways of life and values. It indicates a change in people’s food habits, dress habits, speaking styles, tastes, choices, preferences, ideas, values, and recreational activities.' (Unit 4, Session 6)

questions.question: What is a negative effect of industrialisation?
options: Technological unemployment due to labour-saving devices
questions.explanation: The module: 'Machines not only provide employment opportunities for men but they also take away the job of men through labour-saving devices. This results in technological unemployment.' (Unit 4, Session 6)

questions.question: What is kenkey?
options: A staple food made from corn or maize in Ghana
questions.explanation: The module: 'Kenkey is one of the staple food made from corn or maize in Ghana.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is the first step in preparing kenkey?
options: Soak the dry corn in water
questions.explanation: The module: 'Soak the dry corn in water.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is 'aflata' in kenkey preparation?
options: The cooked part of the fermented dough mixed with raw dough
questions.explanation: The module: 'This half of the dough is called the “aflata”.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is bread?
options: A staple food prepared from a dough of flour and water, usually by baking
questions.explanation: The module: 'Bread is a staple food prepared from a dough of flour and water, usually by baking.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is alcohol?
options: A colourless volatile flammable liquid produced by natural fermentation of sugars
questions.explanation: The module: 'Alcohol is a colourless volatile flammable liquid which is produced by the natural fermentation of sugars.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is Akpeteshie?
options: A local alcohol distilled from palm wine or sugarcane juice
questions.explanation: The module: 'It is distilled from palm wine or sugarcane juice.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: How is Akpeteshie produced?
options: By fermenting palm wine, boiling it, passing vapour through a copper pipe to condense, and collecting the distillate
questions.explanation: The module describes the process: 'The palm wine is first tapped and fermented... fires are built under the barrels... The vapour is passed through a copper pipe... condenses... The boiled juice then undergoes a distillation.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is one contribution of science and technology to food?
options: It has improved preservation, processing, packaging, and distribution
It has made food less safe
questions.explanation: The module: 'Science and technology have improved the diverse ways of managing our food through advanced methods like preservation, processing, packaging, and distribution.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the purpose of pasteurization?
options: To eliminate health hazards from microbial pathogens and extend shelf life
questions.explanation: The module: 'Pasteurization of milk is just one of many examples of processes that eliminate a health hazard for the consumer and extend the life of the product.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the impact of science and technology on healthcare delivery?
options: It improves quality, increases patient safety, decreases medical errors, and strengthens patient-provider interaction
questions.explanation: The module: 'Science and technology have improved the quality of healthcare delivery, increases patient safety, decreases medical errors, and strengthens the interaction between patients and healthcare providers.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is augmented reality used for in surgery?
options: It helps surgeons and their teams perform complex operations
questions.explanation: The module: 'Augmented reality now helps surgeons and their teams perform complex operations.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is one environmental impact of transport?
options: It contributes to global warming through emission of carbon dioxide
questions.explanation: The module: 'transport is a significant contributor to global warming through the emission of carbon dioxide.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the role of sensors in modern vehicles?
options: They help determine the best route, alert to other vehicles, and monitor driver fatigue
questions.explanation: The module: 'Sensors in the vehicle communicate with GPS services to determine the best route... Sensors alert drivers to the position of other vehicles... A series of sensors in the seat belt can track the driver’s physiological indicators and determine whether the driver is fatigued or intoxicated.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: How has the internet affected culture?
options: It has made the universe a global village, but some cultures have consumed others
It has no effect on culture
questions.explanation: The module: 'While the internet has made the universe a global village, some cultures have consumed others.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a positive effect of industrialisation?
options: It has brought about modernisation and improved transport and communication
questions.explanation: The module: 'It has brought about modernisation. ... Improved development of transport and communication.' (Unit 4, Session 6)

questions.question: What are the ingredients for bread?
options: Yeast, water, sugar, salt, butter, flour
questions.explanation: The module lists ingredients: '4½ teaspoons instant yeast, ¾ cup + 2⅔ cups warm water, ¼ cup granulated sugar, 1 tablespoon salt, 3 tablespoons unsalted butter, 9 to 10 cups of all-purpose flour.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is the process of making alcohol from palm wine?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The palm wine is first tapped and fermented... fires are built under the barrels... The vapour is passed through a copper pipe... condenses... The boiled juice then undergoes a distillation.' (Unit 4, Session 7)

questions.question: What is the role of science in the scientific method?
options: It involves making observations, recording data, analysing information, and constructing theoretical explanations
questions.explanation: The module: 'Scientists make observations, record measurable data relating to their observations, and analyse the information at hand to construct theoretical explanations of the phenomenon involved.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is one way technology has improved food safety?
options: By removing health hazards associated with microbial pathogens through processes like pasteurization
questions.explanation: The module: 'Processing operations dealing with raw food materials or ingredients carrying pathogens have significant controls and regulations to detect and inactivate food-borne microorganisms that can cause illness. Pasteurization of milk is just one of many examples.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is one advantage of digital health records?
options: They allow for easy storing, managing, and transmission of data
questions.explanation: The module: 'By this, storing, managing, and transmission of data becomes easy and quick.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is one disadvantage of modern communication?
options: It can be used for cybercrime and malicious programs
questions.explanation: The module: 'ICT is being used by some persons for evil purposes such as cybercrime and malicious programs that can cause severe damage to computers.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the effect of industrialisation on cities?
options: It led to the growth of cities as people moved to industrial areas for employment
It had no effect on cities
questions.explanation: The module: 'The growth of industries has contributed to the growth of cities. As a result of industrialisation, people have started moving toward industrial areas in search of employment. Due to this industrial areas developed into towns and cities.' (Unit 4, Session 6)

questions.question: What is a professional educator's portfolio?
options: A collection of selected evidence that makes a compelling argument for some purpose
A collection of all work done
questions.explanation: The module: 'A professional educator's portfolio is generally defined as a collection of selected evidence that makes a compelling argument for some purpose.' (Unit 5, Introduction)

questions.question: What is reflective practice?
options: Thinking about and critically analyzing one's actions with the goal of improving professional practice
questions.explanation: The module: 'Reflective practice involves thinking about and critically analyzing one's actions with the goal of improving one's professional practice.' (Unit 5, Introduction)

questions.question: What is a portfolio?
options: A purposeful collection of students' work that exhibits the student's efforts, progress, and achievements
questions.explanation: The module: 'A portfolio is a purposeful collection of students’ work that exhibits the student‘s efforts, progress, and achievements in one or more areas.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: Portfolios are often described in terms of:
options: Use – they can be purposeful collections of students' work reflecting efforts and achievements
questions.explanation: The module: 'Portfolios are often described in terms of use: that is they can be purposeful collections of students’ work reflecting efforts and achievements...' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What does a portfolio include besides evidence?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Portfolios can also be seen as mandated dossiers (records) to evaluate performance... and evidence of self-reflection.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What does portfolio development encourage?
options: Reflective thinking at various levels of deliberation on practical teaching matters
questions.explanation: The module: 'It develops reflective thinking at various levels of deliberation on practical teaching matters.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What are the two major categories of a portfolio?
options: Collection of evidence and reflection on what has been learned
questions.explanation: The module: 'In practice, portfolios often consist of two major categories. First, a collection of evidence... and second, a reflection by the pre-service teachers on what has been learned.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What does the portfolio process help pre-service teachers build?
options: A history of their learning including personal goals, work samples, revisions, corrections, and reflections
questions.explanation: The module: 'In the portfolio process, pre-service teachers build a history of their learning including personal goals, work samples, revisions and corrections, and reflections.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: When pre-service teachers reflect on their portfolios, what does it stimulate?
options: New understanding and appreciation of their experiences, recognition of links between different aspects, and formulation of insights for future actions
questions.explanation: The module: 'When pre-service teachers reflect on their portfolios it stimulates them to develop a new understanding and appreciation of their experiences, recognize links between different aspects of these experiences, and formulate insights to be tested in future actions.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is reflective practice?
options: Learning through and from experience towards gaining new insights into self and practice
questions.explanation: The module: 'Reflective practice is learning through and from experience towards gaining new insights into self and practice.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What does reflection allow a pre-service teacher to do?
options: Articulate and investigate personal goals and evaluate progress towards those goals
questions.explanation: The module: 'Reflection allows a pre-service teacher to articulate and investigate personal goals and evaluate his/her progress towards those goals.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What does reflective thinking encourage?
options: Lifelong learning and becoming a reflective practitioner
questions.explanation: The module: 'A reflective individual is likely inclined towards life-long learning and becoming a reflective practitioner.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the cornerstone of the reflective portfolio?
options: The pre-service teacher's self-reflection, fostering metacognition
questions.explanation: The module: 'The pre-service teacher’s self-reflection fosters metacognition, a person’s awareness of his or her thinking, and is the cornerstone of the reflective portfolio.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a reflective journal?
options: A way of thinking critically and analytically about your work in progress, showing how different aspects interconnect
A record of daily activities only
questions.explanation: The module: 'A reflective journal is a way of thinking critically and analytically about your work in progress. It shows how different aspects of your work interconnect.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a reflective journal?
options: A place to write down daily reflection entries to self-reflect and learn from past experiences
questions.explanation: The module: 'A reflective journal is a place to write down your daily reflection entries. It can be something good or bad that happened to you that you can self-reflect and learn from the past experience.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What can a reflective journal help you identify?
options: Important learning events that happened in your life
questions.explanation: The module: 'A reflective journal can help you to identify important learning events that happened in your life.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a reflective journal for students?
options: Notes or papers prepared by students to reflect on what they understand, encouraging self-correcting and self-evaluating skills
A collection of notes from the teacher
questions.explanation: The module: 'Reflective journal notes prepared by students reflect what they understand, this act encourages learning in them. ... a Reflective journal is the notes or papers prepared by students.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: Why should reflective journals be kept continuously?
options: To be effective, students must do their entries continuously throughout the duration of the course
questions.explanation: The module: 'For reflective journals to be effective students must do their entries continuously throughout the duration of the course.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is one reason to write a reflective journal?
options: To make sense of things that happened
questions.explanation: The module: '1. To make sense of things that happened.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is another reason to write a reflective journal?
options: To speculate as to why something is the way it is
questions.explanation: The module: '2. To speculate as to why something is the way it is.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is another reason to write a reflective journal?
options: To align future actions with your reflected values and experiences
To only focus on the future
questions.explanation: The module: '3. To align future actions with your reflected values and experiences.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is another reason to write a reflective journal?
options: To get thoughts and ideas out of your head
questions.explanation: The module: '4. To get thoughts and ideas out of your head.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is another reason to write a reflective journal?
options: To share your thoughts and ideas with others
questions.explanation: The module: '5. To share your thoughts and ideas with others.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: How are reflective journals used in learning?
options: To explore situations from a personal perspective within the context of learning from students' own experiences
questions.explanation: The module: 'Reflective journals are used to explore situations from a personal perspective, but generally within the context of learning from students’ own experiences.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What are the common questions arising from reflection?
options: What happened? Why did it happen? What can be learnt from this for future actions?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Common questions arising from 'reflection' are: 1. What happened? 2. Why did it happen? 3. What can be learnt from this for future actions?' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What are the three W's of reflective writing?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Use the three W’s to write reflectively. The three ‘W’s are What, So What and What next.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: In the What step of reflective writing, what do you do?
options: Recall an event and write it down descriptively
